cards.sentence: I wouldn't say that's anything to brag about because I've been an inch deep in a mile wide.

keyPhrase.text: be an inch deep and a mile wide
keyPhrase.surface: been an inch deep in a mile wide
keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 여러 분야에 걸쳐 있지만 어느 하나도 깊이 파고들지 못한 상태. '넓고 얕은 T자 역의 반대'
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in business discussions to humbly signal that you have broad exposure to a topic but lack deep specialist knowledge — it preempts criticism and sets honest expectations.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson is reflecting on his own track record as a serial entrepreneur and investor. Rather than claiming deep expertise in any single domain, he acknowledges that his portfolio spans many industries without him having mastered any one of them.

vocabulary.meaning: to boast or speak with excessive pride about one's achievements
vocabulary.note: Used informally; 'brag about' is the common colocation in American English.

cards.sentence: So it's just not that exciting to them, which again, going back to fish where the fish are, you don't want to be fighting against the commercial fishermen.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 반응할 가능성이 높은 시장과 고객에 자원을 집중하다. 싸워 이기기 어려운 곳에서 힘 낭비하지 않기
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in strategy or sales conversations to argue for focusing resources on the most receptive channels or customer segments instead of fighting for market share where competition is brutal.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson is advising on how to find the right customers or investors for your business. He argues that targeting the people who are naturally aligned with what you offer — rather than trying to convince skeptics — is the key to efficient growth.

vocabulary.meaning: relating to large-scale profit-driven operations, often implying dominant market players
vocabulary.note: In the fishing metaphor, 'commercial fishermen' represents entrenched, well-resourced competitors.

cards.sentence: And I think they really need to lean into what I call lazy leadership, which is how do I get away from the things I hate as quickly as humanly possible?

keyPhrase.surface: lean into what I call lazy leadership
keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 하기 싫은 일을 최대한 빠르게 위임·제거해 자신이 가장 잘하는 일에만 집중하는 리더십 철학. '게으른 리더십'
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this when advising leaders on delegation: frame offloading draining tasks as intentional design, not laziness, to unlock higher-leverage work.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson is coaching founders who find themselves buried in operational work they dread. He introduces his own management philosophy — 'lazy leadership' — which reframes delegation not as avoidance but as a strategic tool for concentrating your energy where you add the most value.

vocabulary.meaning: within the limits of what a person can possibly do; used to emphasize urgency or effort
vocabulary.note: Always appears in the phrase 'as quickly as humanly possible' to add dramatic emphasis.

cards.sentence: then you can have a wonderful life and build a wonderful company that can scale into the hundreds of millions of dollars if you play your cards right without ever raising money.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 기회와 자원을 영리하게 활용해 원하는 결과를 이끌어내다. 타이밍과 판단력이 핵심
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in pitches or planning discussions to signal confidence in a plan — it implies that success is within reach as long as execution is disciplined.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson is making the case that raising venture capital is not the only path to building a large, successful company. He argues that founders who manage their resources wisely and execute deliberately can reach significant scale entirely on their own terms.

vocabulary.meaning: to grow a business to a significantly larger size, especially in revenue
vocabulary.note: Used here as a verb; 'scale into the hundreds of millions' means reaching that revenue level.

cards.sentence: And there's this great saying, "To a man with a hammer, everything looks like a nail."

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 아는 방법이 하나뿐이면 모든 문제를 그 방법으로만 해결하려 든다. 도구의 편향이 사고를 가린다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in meetings to caution against cognitive bias toward a favorite solution — it signals intellectual humility and opens the floor to alternatives.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson cites this proverb while warning against the tendency to default to a single solution — such as always raising venture capital or always building software — simply because it is the approach you already know. The saying encourages seeking the genuinely right tool for each situation.

cards.sentence: If I tell them an idea in a board meeting and I say, "I really need you to try this," it never works because usually they sandbag it, right?

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 맡긴 일을 대놓고 거부하지 않고 최소한의 노력만 투입해 슬그머니 망쳐버리다. 소극적 저항의 한 형태
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in leadership or management discussions to describe the hidden cost of mandating ideas rather than generating genuine buy-in from your team.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson is explaining why pushing his portfolio company CEOs to execute his ideas from the top down backfires. When leaders feel forced into a direction they do not own, they carry it out half-heartedly — sabotaging results not through open refusal but through lackluster effort.

vocabulary.meaning: a formal gathering of a company's board of directors or key stakeholders to discuss strategy and performance

cards.sentence: And I also love DJing and I was able to analyze the business and luck comes to the prepared mind and it worked out.

keyPhrase.text: luck comes to the prepared mind
keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 행운은 준비된 사람에게 찾아온다. 남이 '운이 좋았다'고 부르는 것은 대부분 치밀한 사전 분석의 결과
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this to reframe a success story in a pitch or retrospective: it signals to investors or partners that your wins are repeatable because they are built on preparation, not chance.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson recounts how his passion for DJing led him to deeply study the economics of the nightclub business before investing in it. What others might call a lucky break, he attributes to having analyzed the opportunity thoroughly in advance — so when the moment arose, he was positioned to seize it.

vocabulary.meaning: to examine something in detail in order to understand it or draw conclusions from it

cards.sentence: one was reframing money to some degree to have it be something that wasn't just about feathering my own nest and making it better.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 자기 이익만을 위해 부와 특권을 쌓다. 종종 타인의 희생을 발판 삼는다는 뉘앙스 포함
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in leadership or culture conversations to flag self-serving behavior, or to contrast it with mission-driven thinking that puts the team or customer first.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson is describing a personal mindset shift around money and motivation. He realized that his drive to earn had been mostly self-serving, and he wanted to reframe wealth as a tool for creating value beyond his own comfort — connecting financial success to a larger purpose.

vocabulary.meaning: consciously changing the way you think about or interpret a concept in order to shift your emotional response or behavior
vocabulary.note: A term popular in psychology and coaching; 'reframe' as a verb means to deliberately adopt a new perspective.

cards.sentence: Because when I was starting out, I would go down every rabbit trail, every infomercial idea I had I would think was genius.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 번뜩이는 아이디어마다 쫓아다니며 본래 목표에서 자꾸 샛길로 빠지다. 초기 창업자의 흔한 집중력 함정
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in retrospectives or mentoring conversations to describe the trap of chasing too many ideas at once — it signals self-awareness and the value of strategic focus.

cards.context: Andrew Wilkinson is reflecting on his early days as an entrepreneur, when his lack of focus led him to pursue any shiny idea that caught his attention. He uses this phrase to contrast his unfocused younger self with the more disciplined, opportunity-filtering approach he developed over time.

vocabulary.meaning: a long-form television advertisement designed to look like a regular program, typically promoting a novelty product
vocabulary.note: Used humorously here to imply the kind of low-quality, get-rich-quick ideas that seem exciting but rarely pan out.

speaker.name: Andrew Wilkinson
speaker.title: Co-founder, Tiny
speaker.titleHistory: Co-founder of Tiny · Investor · Builder of bootstrapped companies
speaker.motif: a compass needle pointing inward and a fortress built from coin and conviction
speaker.introLine: Those who master the art of building without outside voices will shape empires that move to their own rhythm.
speaker.sealLine: Remember: the freedom to build is won not by raising capital, but by choosing focus over chasing every glimmer.
